content:
2 min read
Just now

--

Like millions of others, I fell in love with the daily ritual of playing Wordle. It was simple, engaging, and gave me a reason to text my friends every morning. However, as a hardcore football (soccer) fan, I always felt there was a missed opportunity. I didn’t just want to guess dictionary words — I wanted to test my sports knowledge.
So, I decided to build it myself. Enter Futbolle.com.
The Inspiration
The idea was straightforward: take the addictive, deductive logic of Wordle and apply it to football players. Instead of guessing a 5-letter word, the user has to guess the “Mystery Player of the Day” in 8 tries.
But letters wouldn’t work here. Instead, I used attributes. Every time a user makes a guess, the game gives them green or yellow feedback based on the player’s:

• Nationality

• League

• Club

• Position

• Age
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Going Beyond Just One Game
As I was building the core game, I realized that football trivia is vast. Why stop at just one game mode? I wanted Futbolle to be a complete daily hub for football fans.
I expanded the project to include 8 different game modes, all refreshing at midnight. Some of my favorites include:

• Who’s That?: Guessing a player from a highly blurred image that gets clearer with every wrong guess.

• Connections: Inspired by NYT Connections, where you group 16 players into 4 hidden categories (e.g., “Played for Real Madrid”, “Won the World Cup”).

• Football Bingo: A fast-paced matching game.
The Tech Stack
I wanted the site to be blazing fast, SEO-friendly, and easy to deploy. Here is the stack I chose:

• Framework: Next.js (App Router)

• Styling: Tailwind CSS

• Deployment: Vercel
Next.js was the perfect choice because it allowed me to easily handle the daily midnight resets using server-side logic and API routes. The static generation ensures the site loads instantly, which is crucial for a daily web game where users want to hop in, play for 5 minutes, and leave.
The Biggest Challenge
The hardest part wasn’t the coding — it was the database. Gathering accurate data for thousands of football players, ensuring their current clubs, ages, and nationalities were up to date, required a lot of manual verification and structuring. I had to build a robust JSON structure to handle the daily puzzle generation without the need for a heavy backend database.
Try It Out!
Building Futbolle has been an incredibly fun journey. If you are a football fan, or if you just love daily logic puzzles, I would be thrilled if you gave it a try.
You can play it for free (no sign-up required) here: Futbolle.com
Let me know what your streak is, and if you have any feedback on the UI or difficulty, please leave a comment below!
